text: Abstract This article explores the cultures of public militancy of the revolutionary left in France, Italy and West Germany during the ‘red decade’ of the 1970s. The article begins by examining the initial convergence of strategic and ideological aspects of these militant cultures across borders and examining their common vocabulary of violence and disobedience in relation to the state. The article then argues for the eventual divergence of militant cultures along national lines due to differing historical experiences of civil war, neo-fascist terrorisms and domestic social conflict. Focusing on the Italian case, it is argued that the revolutionary left of Italy was conditioned to shoot by the regularity of fatal police actions to quell demonstrations and this heightened the level of lethal potential on the streets of Italian cities, far more than was the case in France or West Germany. It will be argued here that the tactics and level of activism adopted by radical movements across borders during the ‘red decade’ were conditioned by domestic policing practices and the very thin line that had to be traversed for activism to cross from the level of mass protest into that of the world of terrorism.
